food_subgroup: Legumes and pulses — cooked lentil dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dal Tadka; FOODON_03311434 (lentil food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Lentils, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific Dal Tadka entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 256319004 represents lentil as a substance; no specific prepared Dal Tadka concept exists.

preparation_method: sautéed spices in oil or ghee
main_ingredients: yellow lentils (toor dal or chana dal)
allergens: may contain traces of gluten depending on preparation environment

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Dal Tadka is approximately 250 g (one medium bowl or katori), as typically served in an Indian restaurant as part of a meal with rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Dal, cooked (Toor / Arhar dal, boiled) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked toor dal (arhar dal) used as the primary nutrient base. Dal Tadka is prepared from yellow lentils (toor/chana dal) tempered with ghee and spices; the tempering adds approximately 2–3 g fat per 100 g compared to plain boiled dal. Fat and energy values have been adjusted upward to reflect the ghee/oil tadka component. Sodium adjusted to reflect added salt in restaurant preparation. All other macros retained from ICMR-NIN cooked dal values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Lentils, red, boiled (Atkinson et al.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dal Tadka is prepared from yellow lentils (toor/chana dal). The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for cooked lentils in the range of 29–48 (mean ~39 for red/yellow lentils, boiled). GI = 39 is used as the best analogous estimate for yellow lentil dal. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (39 × 33.75 g carbohydrate in 250 g serving) / 100 = 13.2. gi_category = low per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: Dal Tadka as served in restaurants contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (800 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents a meaningful contribution to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from lentils
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Tadka has a low glycemic index of approximately 39 and provides 3.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, both of which support postprandial blood glucose control, making it a suitable legume-based dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, plant protein, and low saturated fat relative to total energy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal Tadka provides soluble dietary fibre and plant-based protein from lentils, which are associated with LDL cholesterol reduction; the ghee-based tempering contributes modest saturated fat (1.8 g per 100 g), and individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer versions prepared with unsaturated oil rather than ghee.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Tadka is a classic North Indian lentil dish made from yellow lentils — most commonly toor dal (split pigeon peas) or chana dal (split chickpeas) — that are boiled until soft and creamy, then finished with a hot tempering (tadka or tarka) of ghee or oil infused with garlic, cumin seeds, mustard seeds, dried red chillies, and turmeric. The dish has a rich, earthy, mildly spiced flavour with a smooth, thick consistency. It is a staple of Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across the subcontinent and in the Indian diaspora worldwide, including Singapore. Nutritionally, Dal Tadka is a valuable source of plant-based protein (approximately 6.8 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (3.8 g per 100 g), folate, iron, and potassium. Its low glycemic index (approximately 39) makes it particularly suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The dish is naturally vegetarian and gluten-free, though it is not vegan when prepared with ghee. In a clinical context, Dal Tadka is considered a heart-healthy, diabetes-friendly legume dish; the primary nutritional caution in restaurant settings is elevated sodium from added salt. It is typically served with steamed basmati rice, jeera rice, or Indian flatbreads such as roti or naan, and is a common component of a thali (Indian set meal).
common_user_queries: How many calories in dal tadka?
Is dal tadka good for diabetics?
What lentils are used in dal tadka?
Is dal tadka high in protein?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked toor dal data with an upward adjustment for the ghee/oil tadka component. No exact Dal Tadka entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked yellow/red lentils. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact recipe and fat content of the Bombay Grill restaurant preparation are not independently verified.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on ICMR-NIN cooked toor dal (arhar dal) with fat and energy adjusted upward to account for the ghee/oil tadka tempering typical of restaurant-style Dal Tadka. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in lentils and plant-derived ingredients. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in plant-based lentil preparations with ghee at typical serving quantities. 6. GI = 39 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked yellow and red lentils (range 29–48). 7. The food_group field from the input ('Meat and alternatives') has been preserved as lentils are classified under this group in Singapore HPB and ICMR-NIN frameworks as a protein-rich legume alternative to meat. 8. The input food_name 'Bombay -Grill Dal Tadka' has been normalised to the canonical English name 'Dal Tadka'; the restaurant-specific name is retained in aliases.

Added GI = 39 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for cooked yellow lentils.
Populated full ontology section with closest_parent codes for FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT with gap notes.
Added comprehensive health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) in notes.
Documented vitamin_b12_mcg = 0.0 as naturally zero for plant-based food.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are fully consistent.
